coredata.dc:title: A novel and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence assay for carbendazim based on N-doped carbon quantum dots and gold nanocluster nanohybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yue
Xing, Xinxin
Zou, Tong
Wang, Zidong
Zhao, Rongjun
Hong, Ping
Peng, Sijia
Zhang, Xu
Wang, Yude
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescence “turn on” ratiometric fluorescent sensor was employed to determine carbendazim. The sensing process was achieved through the strong fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and gold nanocluster (AuNCs). The photoluminescence intensity of N-CQDs can be deactivated by AuNCs through FRET effect and recovered by the addition of carbendazim. The ratiometric detection of carbendazim is achieved by recording the photoluminescence and second-order Rayleigh scattering (SRS) signal of N-CQDs/AuNCs system. With the introduction of carbendazim to the sensing platform resulted in the photoluminescence and SRS signal of N-CQDS/AuNCs enhancing. UV–vis absorption, Zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime analyses indicate that the fluorescence turn on process can be attributed to the aggregation of AuNCs breaks the FRET process and increases SRS intensity. N-CQDs/AuNCs probe present a good sensitivity and selectivity for carbendazim detection, with two linear response ranges (1−100 μM, 150−1000 μM), low detection limit of 0.83 μM and 37.25 μM. Furthermore, real sample analyses indicate that the as-presented sensor has potentials in carbendazim determination in real sample analyses.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
N doped carbon quantum dots
Au nanocluster

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The quenching mechanism of N-CQDs by AuNCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM mage (inset: HRTEM) (a) and particle distribution (n = 40) (b) of N-CQDs, (c) TEM image and the corresponding (d) particle size distribution (n = 40) of as-synthesized AuNCs.
(a) The XPS spertrum and high resolution spectra of C 1s (b), N 1s (c) and O 1s (d) for N-CQDs.

(a) UV-vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs and AuNCs; the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs. (b) The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs at various excitation wavelengths. (c) The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/AuNCs sensing probe in the presence and absence of carbendazim. (d) Fluorescence decay spectra of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid.

(a) The fluorescence quenching properties of N-CQDs in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of various amounts of AuNCs. (b) The effect of pH on the carbendazim detection. (c) The fluorescence ratios change with the incubation time.

(a) Fluorescence response of N-CQDs/ AuNCs probe in the presence of various concentration of carbendazim (0−1000 μM). (b) The relationship between F/S ratios versus carbendazim concentration. The dynamic response ranges for carbendazim determination over the range of 1–100 μM (c) and 150−1000 μM (d), respectively.

(a) UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs/AuNCs probe in the presence and absence of carbendazim. The fluorescence lifetime decay spectra of N-CQDs/AuNCs before (b) and after the addition of 100 μM (c) and 600 μM (d) of carbendazim. (d) The Zeta potential analyses of N-CQDs, AuNCs and N-CQDs/AuNCs.
TEM images of AuNCs without (a) and with the addition of 100 μM (b) 300 μM (c) and 600 μM (d) of carbendazim, respectively.
The fluorescence ratio (F/S) of as-prepared N-CQDs/AuNCs probe toward various interfering cations and pesticides.
Schematic illustration of N-GQDs/AuNCs as a ratiometric fluorescence turn on sensor for carbendazim.
Comparison of different sensing platforms for the carbendazim detection.
Recovery of carbendazim detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: A novel and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence assay for carbendazim based on N-doped carbon quantum dots and gold nanocluster nanohybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Yunnan University, 650091, Kunming, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Physics, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, People’s Republic of China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, 650091, Kunming, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Quantum Information of Yunnan Province, Yunnan University, 650091, Kunming, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Quantum Information of Yunnan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cKey Lab of Quantum Information of Yunnan Province, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, 650091, Kunming, People’s Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescence “turn on” ratiometric fluorescent sensor was employed to determine carbendazim. The sensing process was achieved through the strong fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and gold nanocluster (AuNCs). The photoluminescence intensity of N-CQDs can be deactivated by AuNCs through FRET effect and recovered by the addition of carbendazim. The ratiometric detection of carbendazim is achieved by recording the photoluminescence and second-order Rayleigh scattering (SRS) signal of N-CQDs/AuNCs system. With the introduction of carbendazim to the sensing platform resulted in the photoluminescence and SRS signal of N-CQDS/AuNCs enhancing. UV–vis absorption, Zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime analyses indicate that the fluorescence turn on process can be attributed to the aggregation of AuNCs breaks the FRET process and increases SRS intensity. N-CQDs/AuNCs probe present a good sensitivity and selectivity for carbendazim detection, with two linear response ranges (1−100 μM, 150−1000 μM), low detection limit of 0.83 μM and 37.25 μM. Furthermore, real sample analyses indicate that the as-presented sensor has potentials in carbendazim determination in real sample analyses.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticide is playing a pivotal role in agriculture. The demands of agricultural products are dramatically increasing with the boosting of human population. In order to ensure the output of agricultural products, pesticides have been widely employed to prevent and control the related diseases of crop (Thiour-Mauprivez et al., 2019). However, pesticide abuse raises serious threat to ecological environment. After years of investigation on the toxicology of pesticide, people have clearly realized the threat of pesticide abuse to ecological system (Sandoval et al., 2019; Miller et al., 2019; Tong et al., 2018). Some reports implied that excessive pesticide residues in food are hazardous to human health (Tsiaoussis et al., 2019). Carbendazim (CBZ) is one of broad-spectrum fungicides, which is effective in the control of diseases caused by fungi (such as imperfect fungi and sac fungi) in various crops (Wang et al., 2009). Besides that carbendazim also have been used as preservative in fruit preservation and orange planting (Jin et al., 2018; Veerappan et al., 2012). Soil has a strong adsorption effect on carbendazim, and carbendazim has a good chemical stability in soil. This situation increase the risk of carbendazim residues in crops and soil, posing a threat to agricultural production and human health (Yang et al., 2017). Although, carbendazim is classified as one of low-toxicity pesticides in normal use (Saien and Khezrianjoo, 2008). But it have been reported that carbendazim can disrupt spermatogenesis and cause a decrease of reproduction in mammals (Liu et al., 2019a). Moreover, carbendazim exposure can impair the liver function leads to hepatic lipid metabolism disorder and alter the enzyme activity in mammals (Veerappan et al., 2012; Jin et al., 2015).

To date, several available methods have been developed to determine carbendazim, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Wu et al., 2009), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (Chen et al., 2019), electrochemistry (Gao et al., 2019) and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS) (Mahdavi et al., 2019). Some of them were found to be intrinsic disadvantages including expensive equipment, complicated procedure of sample with time-consuming pretreatment. Recently, fluorescence based sensor have emerged in pesticide detection field owing to its good sensitivity, fast response and facile detection process (Rubio et al., 2019; Tan et al., 2019). Fluorescent materials are crucial for photoluminescence recording during fluorescent detection process. This requires good quantum yield and stable photoluminescence performance of fluorimetric materials (Wang et al., 2018a).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have drawn increasing attention for its good photoluminescence stability, tunable particle size and good biocompatibility (Ghosh et al., 2019). Based on these properties CQDs can be served as promising candidates in various applications like fluorescent sensor, solar cell, bioimaging, and drug delivery (Molaei, 2019). In terms of fluorescent sensor, fluorescence “turn off” process of CQDs have developed for the heavy metal ion and arsenite determination (Tang et al., 2018; P. D. et al., 2017). The fluorescence turn off detection process is related to the fluorescence quenching of carbon quantum dots by heavy metal ion. Another CQDs-based fluorescence sensor is on the basis of the fluorescence “turn on” performance (Li et al., 2019a; Yang et al., 2018a). In a typical fluorescence turn on detection process, the fluorescence of sensing probe is firstly quenched by quenchers, and then restored by the addition of detection object. CQDs and AuNCs nanohybrid is one of common fluorescence turn on sensing probe in fluorescent sensor (Babaee et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2018). Nevertheless, the nanohybrid fluorescence based sensors are mainly evaluated by measuring single fluorescent peak. The detection results are susceptible to the probe concentration and incident light drift (He et al., 2018). To evade these drawbacks, ratiometric fluorimetry method was developed (Niu et al., 2016). The ratiometric detection process is preferable by recording the ratio of two distinct fluorescence signals. Fluorescence sensing method for carbendazim detection has been developed, but the detection process mainly relies on recording single fluorescence signal (Yang et al., 2018b). Although the detection limit by single fluorescent signal measurement for carbendazim is satisfactory (0.002 μM), but the dynamic range for carbendazim detection is only from 0.005 to 1.57 μM (Yang et al., 2018b). Considering the actual usage situation, wide dynamic response range is as significant as the low detection limit.

Herein, N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid based ratiometric fluorescence sensors were established and applied to detect carbendazim. The ratiometric response of N-CQDs/AuNCs system to carbendazim is implemented by measuring fluorescence and SRS (F/S) intensity ratio. These dual detection signals are dependent on the FRET interaction between carbendazim and AuNCs, deactivating the fluorescence of N-CQDs. With the addition of carbendazim, the interaction between AuNCs and N-CQDs was reduced, resulting the AuNCs aggregation and N-CQDs fluorescence recover. Namely, the aggregation of AuNCs can deactivate the FRET process, resulting the fluorescence and SRS intensity enhancement. Experimental analyses indicate that as-synthesized ratiometric fluorescent platform have good detection performance on carbendazim with a wide linear response range from 1 to 1000 μM.

Citric acid, sodium hydroxide and ammonium hydroxide were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). Chloroauric acid was purchased from Sino-Precious Metals Holding Co. Ltd. Carbendazim was supplied by Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai). The interfering tests metal salts were obtained from commercial source. The interfering pesticides were supplied by Jiangsu Pesticide Research Institute. All the reagents were analytical grade and used directly.

N-CQDs were generated by hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1 g citric acid and 1 mL of ammonium hydroxide were mixed and dissolved in 60 mL water with continually stirring until the reagents were completely dissolved. Then, the mixed solution was put into a Teflon-sealed autoclave and incubated at 190 °C for 10 h. The obtained brown solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpms) and collected. After that the supernatant was dialyzed against ultra-pure water for 12 h by using dialysis bag (MWCO: 300 Da). Finally, the resulting solution was stored at 4 °C for further experiment.

AuNCs were carried out using a previous report with minor modification (Yang et al., 2019). Specifically, 1 mL of 90 mM HAuCl4, 10 mL of L-glutathione (10 mM) solution and 49 mL of ultrapure water were mixed together. After that, the mixed solution was incubated to 95 °C in an oil bath and gently stirred for 2 h. Then the supernatant was centrifuged at 1000 rpms to remove large particles. The final supernatant was stored in refrigerator for the following experiment.

JEOL-JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) was applied to investigate the morphology of AuNCs and N-CQDs. UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs and AuNCs were collected by a UV–vis-1800 spectrophotometer (Jinghua Instrument, China). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded on K-Alpha+ X-ray electron spectrometer. The fluorescence and fluorescence lifetime were measured by a Horiba Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer and Quantaurus-Tau fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Hamamatsu, Japan). An Avatar-360 spectrometer was applied to record Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FT-IR) to investigate the functional group information of as-synthesized carbon quantum dots. The HPLC measurement was carried out by Agilent 1100 liquid chromatograph, with a DAD detector and Eclipse XDB-C18 column (250 × 4.6 mm, Agilent, USA).

The fluorescence probe was first prepared as described above: 100 μL of N-CQDs (10 μg/mL) and 400 μL of AuNCs were injected into a centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of 4.3 mL PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH 6). The resulting solution were shaken thoroughly and incubated for 5 min. After that, 200 μL carbendazim was mixed with the abovementioned mixtures and equilibrated for 10 min. The analytical performances of as-prepared fluorescence probe were investigated by recording the photoluminescence spectra of N-CQDs and SRS intensity of sensing system. The ratiometric fluorescence assay of N-CQDs/AuNCs on carbendazim was carried out by the fluorescence (F) and SRS intensity (S) ratios (F/S) of as-prepared sensing probe, respectively. The selectivity of the proposed sensing system was carried out by addition various interfering cations and pesticides into the fluorescence probe. The excited wavelength was set as 360 nm for all of the fluorescent measurements and the of emission slit width was set as 3 nm.

The feasibility of as-prepared ratiometric fluorescence probe was evaluated by determine carbendazim in apple and strawberry samples. Apple and strawberry for real sample analyses were obtained from local market. Certain concentrations of carbendazim were sprayed onto the surface of fruits and left to dry in air. Then a blender was applied to juice the fruit sprayed with carbendazim and the following sample treatment procedure was prepared according to previous study (Yang et al., 2018b; Li et al., 2016). 5 g of fruit puree samples were mixed with 10 mL dichloromethane and ultrasonication for 2 min. Then the obtained solution was centrifuged (8000 rpms) to remove the insoluble substance and incubated for 10 min at 50 ℃, followed by the addition of DMF/Water (1:1) mixture and diluted to 15 mL. After that, 200 μL of the resulting solution were mixed with the fluorescence probe and equilibrated for 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the real samples were measured to detect carbendazim.

The nanostructure information of as-synthesized N-CQDs and AuNCs was firstly characterized by TEM measurement. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the as-synthesized N-CQDs present monodispersed dispersion. From the HRTEM image of N-CQDs, a clear lattice configuration was observed with a carbon lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which resemble the (100) graphite facet (Zhang et al., 2019). The particle distribution analyses (Fig. 1(b)) of N-CQDs demonstrate a narrow diameter with a mean particle diameter of 4.5 nm. As presented in Fig. 1(c) and (d), morphology and particle distribution investigations of as-synthesized AuNCs indicate that the AuNCs are well-dispersed with a mean particle size of 2.2 nm.

The elemental composition information of N-CQDs were investigated by XPS analyses and given in Fig. 2 . The C1 s, N1 s and O1 s signals can be observed at 286, 400 and 530 eV in Fig. 2(a). The C1 s signal (Fig. 2(b)) reveals four peaks, corresponding to the contribution of CC (285.8 eV), CN (286.9 eV), CO/CH (288.6 eV) and OCO (289.5 eV) (Wang et al., 2018b). As plotted in Fig. 2(c), the fitted peaks of N1 s were ascribed to the contribution of CN (399.7 eV), CNC (400.5 eV) and OCN (402.1 eV) (Niu et al., 2016; Zheng et al., 2018). Three deconvoluted peaks at 531.7, 532.3 and 533.7 eV can be observed in O1 s high-resolution spectra. These peaks were contributed from the OH, CO and CO binding energy, respectively (Sangam et al., 2018).

In addition, the surface groups of N-CQDs were characterized by FT-IR measurement. As plotted in Fig. 3 , the stretching vibration OH/NH is observed around 3196 cm−1. The absorption peak around 1712 cm−1 belong to the vibration of CO (Li et al., 2017). The presence of OH and CO absorption bands suggest that there are abundant oxygen-containing groups on N-CQDs. In addition, the absorption band around 1663, and 1403 cm−1 are contributed from CC and OCNH vibration, respectively (Rong et al., 2018). The absorption band of CN and CN vibration can be observed at 1570 and 1192 cm−1, relating to the doped N atoms.

The optical information of N-CQDs and AuNCs was carried out by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence measurement. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 (a), the red and green curves are related to the UV–vis measurements of as-synthesized N-CQDs and AuNCs, respectively. There is an obvious absorption peak around 329 nm in N-CQDs spectrum, corresponding to the n-π* transition of CO (Liu et al., 2017). The absorption of AuNCs demonstrates a strong absorption in UV region. The photoluminescence spectra of N-CQDs indicate that the strongest emission intensity of N-CQDs at 450 nm (cyan curve) is excited by 360 nm (blue curve) excited light. Fig. 4(b) displays the emission spectra of N-CQDs associated with various excited illumination. It was observed that the emission peak position of N-CQDs remains basically in the same position under different excitation wavelength, which can be assigned to the narrow diameter dispersion of as-synthesized carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching mechanism of N-CQDs by AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Fig. 4(c), the fluorescent signal of N-CQDs is decreased in the presence of AuNCs and restored after the addition of carbendazim. It have been reported that AuNCs can be served as an effective quencher for carbon quantum dots by FRET (Li et al., 2019b). In a typical FRET process, the energy transfer between fluorescence materials and quencher is based on the absorption bands of quenchers overlap with the emission band of fluorescence materials (Zu et al., 2017). As you can see in Fig. 4(a), the absorption band of AuNCs has overlaps with the excitation and emission of N-CQDs. The possible quenching mechanism might be attributed to the FRET process. To further elucidate the possible quenching process, the fluorescence lifetime decay spectra were recorded (Fig. 4(d)). The fluorescence life time changes are related to the energy transfer between fluorescence materials and quencher. In order to determine the fluorescence life time decay, the average fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs were given and depicted in Fig. 4(d). The average lifetime of N-CQDs decreased from 5.53 to 3.13 ns in the presence of AuNCs. This result indicates that there is electron transfer from N-CQDs to AuNCs. Therefore, the FRET effect between AuNCs and N-CQDs result in the fluorescence quenching.

The fluorescence turn on analytical performance of N-CQDs/AuNCs is based on the interaction between N-CQDs and AuNCs. In order to obtain a good analytical performance, the pH, reaction time and the amount of AuNCs need to be considered. The analytical performance of N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid was carried out by measuring the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs and SRS intensity ratio (F/S). The amount of AuNCs is important for interaction between N-CQDs and AuNCs, which is related to the FRET fluorescence quenching process. Fig. 5 (a) displays the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) of N-CQDs changes with the introduction of AuNCs, the fluorescence quenching efficiency boosts with the addition of AuNCs. When the addition amounts of AuNCs reach to 400 μL, the fluorescence quenching efficiency is stable. Thereby, 400 μL of AuNCs was mixed with N-CQDs in the following measurement.

Fig. 5(b) displays the pH dependent photoluminescence performance of as-prepared N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. The fluorescent intensity of N-CQDs presents an increment from pH 2 to pH 7 and remains stable in alkaline condition. This situation is related to the protonation process of amino and carboxyl groups on N-CQDs in acidic condition, leading to the aggregation of N-CQDs with weak fluorescence (Wang et al., 2017). The fluorescence difference of N-CQDs after the addition of carbendazim is increasing from pH 2 to pH 6. At a low pH value, the carboxyl groups on AuNCs are protonated, reducing the FRET effect between N-CQDs and AuNCs (Yang et al., 2018b). This result leads to weak fluorescence increase after the injection of carbendazim under acidic condition. Furthermore, carbendazim would decompose in alkaline conditions, resulting in the fluorescence enhancement reduction. When the pH values of the medium reach to 6, the fluorescence enhancement of as-developed probe displays the maximum. To gain a good detection performance, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at pH 6.

The incubation time is one of crucial conditions for rapid sensing procedure and the optimization of incubation time is plotted in Fig. 5(c). The F/S values remain stable after the addition of carbendazim for 8 min, indicating the sensor probe is ready for work. The ratios of F/S increase dramatically after the addition of carbendazim in the first 8 min. Therefore, the fluorescence intensities were recorded after 10 min of incubation in the following measurement.

The fluorescence turn on ratiometric probe for carbendazim is based on the high FRET efficiency. After optimized the above mentioned pH, incubation time and AuNCs addition amount, the as-developed N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid ratiometric fluorescence probe were then applied to detect carbendazim. The ratiometric fluorescence assay for carbendazim is evaluated by recording the fluorescence and SRS signal of N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. Fig. 6 (a) shows the photoluminescence spectra of N-CQDs/AuNCs changes with various concentration of carbendazim. Two obvious peaks can be observed, corresponding to the fluorescent peak of N-CQDs around 450 nm and SRS signal of N-CQDs/AuNCs around 724 nm, respectively. The weak fluorescence peaks around 630 nm is form the contribution of AuNCs. As is clearly seen, the fluorescence peaks of N-CQDs are gradually enhancing after the injection of carbendazim. These phenomena indicate that the FRET interactions between N-CQDs and AuNCs are decaying after the addition of carbendazim. It is noticeable that the intensities of SRS signal also increase with the increase of carbendazim concentration. According to the Rayleigh scattering theory, the scattering signals are related to the radius of scatter and the SRS light scattering intensities is far weaker than Rayleigh scattering (Liu et al., 2019b). The SRS signal intensities is increasing with the addition of carbendazim, indicating the particle size of as-developed sensing probe is larger than the initial size.

To assess the feasibility of as-synthesized ratiometric fluorescence probe for carbendazim assay, the fluorescence intensity and SRS signal ratio of N-CQDs/AuNCs is measured and plotted in Fig. 6(b). The value of fluorescence and SRS signal ratio is decreasing with the increase of carbendazim concentration (Fig. 6(b)). A linear dynamic response range can be obtained from 1 μM to 100 μM (Fig. 6(c)) with the calibration equation of F/S = 22.73985–0.17335 [CBZ] (R2 = 0.993), the corresponding detection limit is calculated as 0.83 μM (0.15 mg/kg, S/N = 3). Furthermore, another dynamic response range from 150 μM to 1000 μM can be obtained from Fig. 6(d), with low detection limit as 37.25 μM (7.12 mg/kg, F/S = 5.3892-0.00382 [CBZ], R2 = 0.994)). According to the China Food and Drug Administration regulations for food safety stand, the threshold residue limits for carbendazim in fruit (GB 2763-2016) is 1 mg/kg (Maximum residue limites for peticides in food, 2019). And the recommends maximum allowable residue levels of carbendazim from World Health Organization are 5 mg/kg (Jin et al., 2018). Table 1 summarized some proposed methods for carbendazim detection. It can be seen that the as-prepared fluorescence probe has good analytical performance on carbendazim detection. As compared in Table 1, the as-developed ratiometric fluorescence probe presents a wide response range for carbendazim detection. Although the determination for carbendazim established by Ma et al. presented a comparable dynamic range (26–1050 μM), but the actual detection concentration of carbendazim started with 26 μM (Ma et al., 2017a). The detection of carbendazim under 26 μM might be unreliable. Furthermore, the preparation of the polymer sensor requires poisonous reactants and solvents. Hou et al. established a fluorescent probe for carbendazim detection with a low detection limits as 0.002 μM. However, the dynamic response range is only from 0.005 to 1.57 μM, which is unsuitable for the detection around the safe residue level (1 mg/kg, 5.23 μM). Therefore, the as-prepared N-CQDs/AuNCs ratiometric fluorescence probe show a competitive detection performance on carbendazim and it can be served as a candidate for the carbendazim detection.

In this work, the ratiometric fluorescence probe is consisted of two different fluorescent nanoparticle N-CQDs and AuNCs. The determination of carbendazim is carried out by the fluorescence turn on process of as-prepared sensing probe. The fluorescence peak of N-CQDs around 450 nm can be quenched by AuNCs through FRET effect, and recovered by adding carbendazim. For the purpose of confirming the response mechanism of N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid fluorescence probe, the fluorescence lifetime decay, UV–vis absorption and Zeta potentials of the as-developed fluorescence probe was recorded. As plotted in Fig. 7 (a), the UV–vis absorption spectra only changed slightly without new absorption peak generation. It turns out that there is no new complex formed during the interaction process between carbendazim and N-CQDs/AuNCs. To further investigate the interaction between carbendazim and as-prepared sensing probe, fluorescence lifetime decay of N-CQDs/AuNCs were measured and shown in Fig. 7(b) and (c). The average lifetime of as-prepared probe increased from 3.15 ns to 4.98 ns with the addition of 100 and 600 μM carbendazim, respectively. This result indicates that the FRET process between N-CQDs and AuNCs is reduced in the presence of carbendazim. In addition, Zeta potentials were carried out to determine the stabilities of N-CQDs and AuNCs (Zan et al., 2018). The Zeta potential values of N-CQDs and AuNCs (Fig. 7(d)) have negative values, which can be assigned to the negative charge distribution on the surface of as-synthesized AuNCs and N-CQDs. The negative charge distribution is related to the contribution of surface carboxyl groups. It worth to notice that the Zeta potential values of as-prepared sensing probe increased after the addition of carbendazim, which indicate that the stability of as-prepared sensing probe is reduced in the presence of carbendazim and lead to effective aggregation. Zeta potential analyses of carbendazim and N-CQDs in the presence and absence of carbendazim were recorded and shown in Fig. S1. The Zeta potential values of N-CQDs are only change slightly, which indicate that N-CQDs are stable after the addition of carbendazim. The fluorescence intensity of as-prepared sensing probe can be enhanced by the addition of carbendazim, this phenomenon may be related to the aggregation of AuNCs, reducing the FRET effect between N-CQDs and AuNCs. The aggregation of AuNCs can be attested by TEM analysis (Fig. 8 ). As displayed in Fig. 8, an obvious aggregation of AuNCs can be seen after the addition of carbendazim, and the aggregation process present carbendazim concentration dependent property. In order to further investigate the fluorescence turn on mechanism for carbendazim detection, the fluorescence spectrum of AuNCs in the presence and absence of carbendazim were recorded and shown in Fig. S2. The results indicate that the SRS signals are mainly contributed from AuNCs signals. Therefore, the fluorescence turn on determination mechanism for carbendazim was shown in Scheme 1 . The fluorescence signals of N-CQDs/AuNCs were quenched by the FRET process between N-CQDs and AuNCs. The fluorescence signal of N-CQDs can be restored after the addition of carbendazim. The fluorescence turn on response process for carbendazim detection is related to the aggregation of AuNCs, which reduce the energy transfer between AuNCs and N-CQDs, lead to the enhancement of N-CQDs fluorescence.

Selectivity investigation is crucial and necessary for fluorescent sensor. The selectivity of N-CQDs /AuNCs nanohybrid probe was carried out by recording the fluorescence and SRS intensity ratio (F/S) and given in Fig. 9 . In order to elucidate the selectivity of as-synthesized fluorescence turn on probe, various possible interfering cations and pesticides were taken into consideration, including K+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Na+, thiamethoxam, acetamiprid, fluazinam, thriadimefon, dipterex, dursban, imidacloprid and dinotefuran. As shown in Fig. 9, these aforementioned substances only have slightly interference on the detection process. Only carbendazim can enhance the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs with decent F/S ratio. The recognition of N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid probe on carbendazim can be attributed to the special interaction between AuNCs and carbendazim. Some reports have shown that the aromatic configuration and nitrogen atoms in carbendazim have good affinity to AuNCs (Yang et al., 2018b; Ma et al., 2017b). As illustrated in Scheme 1, the good affinities between AuNCs, aromatic configuration and nitrogen atoms are conductive to the adsorption of carbendazim on the surface of AuNCs. The distance between AuNCs can be narrowed by the adsorption of carbendazim and leads to the aggregation of AuNCs. Therefore, the as-synthesized fluorescence probe presents a good sensitivity and selectivity on carbendazim.

The practical application of as-prepared N-CQDs/AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent sensor is evaluated by detecting carbendazim in strawberry and apple. Certain concentrations of carbendazim (30 μM, 50 μM, and 200 μM) were spiked into the samples and the photoluminescence spectra were measured as aforesaid condition. In addition, the recoveries of real sample detection are calculated and listed in Table 2 . The recoveries of strawberry and apple analyses are ranging from 83 % to 120 % and 92%–107%, respectively. The fluorescence detection performances of as-prepared N-CQDs/AuNCs probe were compared with HPLC analyses (Table 2). The results imply that the as-developed ratiometric fluorescence probe has potential application for carbendazim determination in fruit samples.

In summary, a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence probe is developed and applied to detect carbendazim based on the N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. The ratiometric determination process is based on the FRET effect between AuNCs and N-CQDs. The fluorescence of N-CQDs is quenched by AuNCs and restored by the addition of carbendazim. The aggregation of AuNCs reduces the FRET effect, leading to the SRS signal enhance and N-CQDs fluorescence recover. Consequently, the as-prepared N-CQDs/AuNCs were successfully applied to detect carbendazim ranging from 1 to 100 μM and 150 to 1000 μM, with a low detection limit as 0.83 μM and 37.25 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the as-prepared sensing probe was served as a sensitive fluorescence probe to determine carbendazim in real samples. All these results demonstrate that N-CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid is a promising candidate for ratiometric sensing.
Yue Yang, synthesized the samples, performed PL experiments, and wrote the main manuscript.
Xinxin Xing and Tong Zou, analyzed the data.
Zidong Wang, Rongjun Zhao, Ping Hong, Sijia Peng, Xu Zhang, performed XPS, PL and TEM experiments.
Yude Wang, revised and edited the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Department of Science and Technology of Yunnan Province via the Key Project for the Science and Technology (Grant No. 2017FA025) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61761047 and No. 41876055) and Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Yunnan Province. This work also Supported by Yunnan University’s Research Innovation Fund for Graduate Students (Grant No. 2019z036).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.121958.
